    11. Why You Need A 'Weapon List'
</h2>

  <!-- Attachment Blocks -->
<!-- DEBUG: attachments.count=4 -->

  <!-- DEBUG: attachment.id=56857970, kind=text -->
  <div class="lecture-attachment lecture-attachment-type-text" id="lecture-attachment-56857970">
    <div class="attachment-data"></div>
    
      <div class="attachment-data"></div>
<div class="lecture-text-container">
<p></p><p>I got this email from a student a while ago:</p><h3><strong>Dear Father Bronques, even though I just got signed to a record deal, I'm having crazy doubts. I read this amazing biography about Kate Bush recently, and I started feeling like maybe I'm kind of 'late to the party'? I don't know of a lot of 26-year olds that still have yet to release their first project, and still become successful. It seems like it's a young person's game. Rationally, I know these thoughts are stupid and unproductive. And that "I can become anything you imagine". But how can I delete these negative thoughts that persist in my brain when I see people like Billie Eillish out there? My ego keeps pounding me with thoughts like: "give it up, you're too late darling!" Do you have any advice for conquering these toxic thoughts that keep on popping up in my head every now and then?</strong></h3><p><br></p><p>MY REPLY:</p><p>You know what another student did recently?</p><p>He wrote down all the things that he had working against him art-wise. Every negative thing he could think of. He didn't hold back.</p><p>And then he sent me the list "for accountability".</p><p>It was his way of facing the truth. To stay realistic as he continued to forge ahead anyways.</p><p>I loved his determination.</p><p>But when I got it, it seemed incomplete to me.</p><p>So I emailed him back and I asked him to make ANOTHER list.</p><p>In this list I wanted him to explain to me... REALISTICALLY... why each of the statements he had made were FALSE.</p><p>A few days later he emerged with it:</p><p></p>
</div>

<div class="lecture-text-container">
<p></p><p>That's his WEAPON LIST now. He looks at it whenever the "stupid thoughts" creep in. He has ammunition now.</p><p>I suggest you do the same.</p><p>Let me start you off...</p><p>James Murphy STARTED LCD Soundsystem when he was 35. And influenced a whole generation younger than him.</p><p>Peaches started making music when she was 32.</p><p>2 Chainz made his mark in hip hop when he was 34.</p><p>You're 26! Enjoy the cross-section of wisdom and youth (it could last another 40 years if you play your card right).</p><p><br></p><h3><strong>***Everyone should make a weapon list. That way you have a solid list of REALISTIC responses to your negative thoughts. 'Cause when you're in the moment, feeling low, the good combacks and uplifting responses don't tend to come as quick. It may be hard to remember why you're amazing. Or why you're blessed. Make this your ammunition. Sometimes just having it there is enough to keep the "stupid thoughts" away.</strong></h3><p></p>
</div>

      <div data-original-comment-body="Age is one of my insecurities and consistent battles on this journey…it’s almost weekly. My counter to this emotional resistance is doing the work and staying true to myself. I also exploit my age using life experiences in my art (my current vibe is: I don’t have to be a master of the medium just the message*). I respect, admire and remain open minded to the advice from those who gone before me young &amp; old (I see us as teammates on this journey to “win”). I also look at those who started art at an early age, who are successful (and not), as older in their art-age since they went down a different life path than me initially. 
Nevertheless, I feel all the trials and tribulations of a young artist at an old age. So while humbling &amp; challenging at times I believe that success is within reach no matter the age. Just have to put in the work to override the resistance…I also have a weapons list in the form of a mind map.
